I am not sure whether this is something my broadband provider needs to handle or whether I need to go through my landlord or building management.
Please figure out the right route for this problem.

content.content:
content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Landlords - Multi dwelling unit | Openreach

  - url: https://www.openreach.com/help-and-support/landlords-multi-dwelling-unit

  - source: Openreach

  - domain: openreach.com


  ## Content


  # Landlords - Multi dwelling unit


  Full Fibre work in blocks of flats and apartment buildings can involve residents,
  broadband service providers, Openreach, landlords, freeholders and managing agents.
  The right contact depends on the type of service issue and whether resolving it
  needs a new building-level permission, access arrangement or coordination step.


  ## Individual service repairs and shared building areas


  A fault should not be routed only from the physical location of the affected cable
  or equipment. Network apparatus can run through communal parts of an apartment building,
  and an individual resident''s broadband order or repair is still managed through
  the resident''s broadband service provider.


  For an individual service problem, the resident should use the broadband service
  provider''s repair process when the affected network apparatus is already covered
  by an existing maintenance-access arrangement and no new landlord or managing-agent
  action is needed. The service provider remains the customer-facing route for checking
  the fault, arranging repair work and keeping the customer informed.


  The fact that a failed segment is between the building entry point and a flat, or
  that engineers may work in a communal area, does not by itself make the fault a
  landlord matter.


  ## When the landlord or building manager becomes the relevant route


  The landlord, freeholder or managing agent becomes directly relevant when the repair
  cannot proceed without building-level action. Examples include:


  - a new permission or access arrangement is needed before engineers can enter a
  communal area;

  - an existing access agreement does not cover the required work;

  - the building must coordinate entry to a locked shared space;

  - several residents or shared building facilities must be coordinated before work
  can continue;

  - the problem concerns infrastructure or facilities that are maintained through
  the building rather than the resident''s individual service repair process.


  In those cases, the building-management route is needed because the unresolved dependency
  sits with the property or managing agent, not merely because the fault is physically
  inside the building.


  ## Wayleaves and maintenance access


  Apartment blocks may have wayleave or other access arrangements covering network
  apparatus on private property or in communal parts of the building. These arrangements
  can allow network equipment to be installed, maintained or repaired without starting
  a new permission process for every individual fault.


  A wayleave is an access instrument, not by itself a customer-facing support destination.
  The route can be selected only after checking both whether the current repair is
  covered by that arrangement and whether any new property-level permission, access
  or coordination dependency remains open.


  When a current maintenance record shows that the affected network segment is already
  covered by an existing access arrangement, the resident should not assume that a
  new landlord request is required. The remaining repair should normally stay in the
  resident''s broadband service-provider process unless another building-level dependency
  is specifically identified.


  If the maintenance record instead shows that access is not covered and a new property-level
  action is required, the landlord or managing agent must become involved before the
  repair can progress.


  ## Fault-management responsibilities in a multi-dwelling unit


  Landlords and managing agents can help with:


  - providing or arranging access to communal areas when it is not already available;

  - coordinating communication where several residents are affected;

  - helping engineers reach shared spaces controlled by the building;

  - maintaining records of building-level issues and access arrangements;

  - supporting repair work that genuinely depends on property-management cooperation.


  These responsibilities do not replace the broadband service provider''s role for
  ordinary individual service repairs. Residents normally report and manage their
  own broadband fault through their chosen service provider.


  ## Choosing the route


  Use the current diagnostic and maintenance-access record together:


  1. Identify the segment where the connection fails.

  2. Check whether that segment is already covered by an existing network maintenance-access
  arrangement.

  3. Check whether any new building-level permission, shared-area access request or
  multi-unit coordination is still unresolved.

  4. If the affected segment is already covered for maintenance and no new building
  dependency is open, use the broadband service-provider repair route.

  5. If the repair is blocked by a property-level permission, access or coordination
  dependency, use the landlord or building-management route for that dependency.


  The correct destination therefore depends on the current maintenance and access
  condition, not on a simple rule that everything outside the flat belongs to the
  provider or everything inside an apartment building belongs to the landlord.

  '

content.content:
The correct route is **your broadband provider, Example Broadband UK—not your landlord or building management**.

Why:

- Diagnostics show the external provider feed reaches the building, but the fault is on the **shared building distribution** between the communal distribution point and your flat.
- The segment is covered by an **active maintenance-access arrangement (wayleave)**.
- There is **no outstanding need for new permission, communal-area access, or building coordination**.
- There is no known area-wide outage, and your router is powered and responding locally.

Openreach’s guidance says that an individual broadband fault remains with the broadband provider even when the failed equipment is in a communal area, provided existing maintenance access covers it.

**Next step:** Report the service as offline to **Example Broadband UK** through its normal repair/support channel and provide this address:

> Flat 14, Alder Court, 27 Wren Street, London N1 6QR

Tell them that diagnostics indicate a failure in the **shared building distribution between the communal distribution point and the flat handoff**. Your provider should open and manage the repair.

You only need to involve the landlord or building manager if your provider later confirms that access to a locked communal area or another building-level dependency is preventing the repair.

final_answer:
The correct route is **your broadband provider, Example Broadband UK—not your landlord or building management**.

Why:

- Diagnostics show the external provider feed reaches the building, but the fault is on the **shared building distribution** between the communal distribution point and your flat.
- The segment is covered by an **active maintenance-access arrangement (wayleave)**.
- There is **no outstanding need for new permission, communal-area access, or building coordination**.
- There is no known area-wide outage, and your router is powered and responding locally.

Openreach’s guidance says that an individual broadband fault remains with the broadband provider even when the failed equipment is in a communal area, provided existing maintenance access covers it.

**Next step:** Report the service as offline to **Example Broadband UK** through its normal repair/support channel and provide this address:

> Flat 14, Alder Court, 27 Wren Street, London N1 6QR

Tell them that diagnostics indicate a failure in the **shared building distribution between the communal distribution point and the flat handoff**. Your provider should open and manage the repair.

You only need to involve the landlord or building manager if your provider later confirms that access to a locked communal area or another building-level dependency is preventing the repair.
